perturbation.original_text: MediWound shall make payment for each Bromelain SP batch that was supplied by CBC on a [***] days basis as of the date of delivery of the applicable batch at MediWound, provided that MediWound has provided CBC with an Acceptance Batch Notice for such purchased batch pursuant to Section 6.4.
perturbation.changed_text: MediWound shall make payment for each Bromelain SP batch that was supplied by CBC on a 180 days basis as of the date of delivery of the applicable batch at MediWound, provided that MediWound has provided CBC with an Acceptance Batch Notice for such purchased batch pursuant to Section 6.4.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment terms to 180 days could violate prompt payment laws if CBC is considered a small business. Laws often exist to ensure timely payments to vendors.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York State Finance Law § 18-b mandates that state agencies pay small businesses within 30 days (or offer valid reasons for delay). Changing the payment term to 180 days violates this statute if CBC qualifies as a small business and MediWound is acting as a state agency.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment terms from an unspecified number of days to 180 days. The Law Explanation states that New York State Finance Law § 18-b mandates payment to small businesses within 30 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this change may violate prompt payment laws, especially if CBC is considered a small business. Since the scraped snippets are unavailable, the Law and Perturbation Explanations combined with the modified text, indicate a contradiction because 180 days is significantly longer than the legally suggested 30 days for small businesses, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable or illegal, thus aligning with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: CBC shall inform MediWound of any announced regulatory inspections that directly involve the Bromelain SP or the Product within 48 hours of the notification to CBC of such an inspection.
perturbation.changed_text: CBC shall inform MediWound of any announced regulatory inspections that directly involve the Bromelain SP or the Product within 7 days of the notification to CBC of such an inspection.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying notification of regulatory inspections could violate cGMP (Current Good Manufacturing Practice) regulations. FDA guidelines require prompt reporting to ensure timely corrective actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180 requires written procedures for reporting regulatory inspections. A 7-day delay in informing MediWound impedes timely corrective action and violates this regulation, which mandates prompt communication of significant findings.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the notification timeframe for regulatory inspections from 48 hours to 7 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that this delay could violate cGMP regulations requiring prompt reporting to ensure timely corrective actions under 21 CFR § 211.180. While the provided snippets do not contain the full text of 21 CFR § 211.180, the Law Explanation asserts it mandates prompt communication, and the snippets provided concern the Code of Federal Regulations Title 21. The delay directly contradicts the requirement for prompt notification.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality and non-use obligations contained in this Agreement shall continue for the duration of this Agreement and for a period of 5 (five) years after termination or expiry of this Agreement, provided however that any Confidential Information with respect to the Product, including without limiting, such information with respect to intellectual property rights in connection with and/or related to the Products shall remain confidential in perpetuity.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality and non-use obligations contained in this Agreement shall continue for the duration of this Agreement and for a period of 1 (one) year after termination or expiry of this Agreement, provided however that any Confidential Information with respect to the Product, including without limiting, such information with respect to intellectual property rights in connection with and/or related to the Products shall remain confidential for a period of 1 (one) year.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to only one year after termination may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially intellectual property, potentially conflicting with trade secret law. A longer duration is typically needed to prevent reverse engineering and maintain competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Limiting confidentiality to one year may not provide adequate protection against reverse engineering or competitive use of sensitive information, potentially enabling the theft of trade secrets, especially considering the nature of trade secrets is confidential for an indefinite period of time.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period for intellectual property related to the product from perpetuity to one year. The Law Explanation states that 18 U.S.C. § 1836 protects trade secrets from misappropriation and that limiting confidentiality to one year may not provide adequate protection against reverse engineering. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which notes that this change may be insufficient to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1836 as dealing with civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets. By reducing the confidentiality period, the changed text potentially weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction by increasing the risk of misappropriation.
